skill.name: observability-monitoring-slo-implement
skill.description: Design service level objectives, select service level indicators, define error budgets, and create monitoring, alerting, reporting, and governance workflows for reliability programs.
skill.summary: Implement practical SLO frameworks with SLIs, error budgets, dashboards, alerts, and governance practices.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and obfuscation alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline code, SLO reliability terminology, CSS, and numeric SLO examples. The only confirmed static risk is hardcoded placeholder URLs in sample SLO-as-code annotations, with no active network request or exfiltration behavior. A low-severity reuse concern remains because sample PromQL and HTML templates interpolate values without documenting validation or escaping.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fences and inline resource references, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation. No executable command runner, shell call, or user-controlled command construction was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown delimiters such as fenced python or yaml blocks, plus inline file references in SKILL.md. Context confirms they are documentation syntax rather than executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Match Reliability Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings occur in SLO guidance, class names, descriptions, targets, and alert text. No cryptographic API, hashing operation, key handling, or security-sensitive algorithm selection was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is an observability playbook, and the flagged lines describe service reliability concepts. Searches found no md5, sha1, crypto library, or password handling evidence.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Prose and CSS
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings point to safety guidance and CSS table styling, not host discovery, process listing, or environment inspection. No evidence found of system inventory, credential discovery, or local machine probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are natural-language guidance and generated report styling. They do not contain commands or APIs that inspect the runtime system.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Placeholder URLs in Example Configuration
low_findings.description: The playbook includes example runbook and Grafana dashboard URLs in a returned YAML template. These URLs are placeholders and are not fetched, but users should replace them with approved internal links before deployment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are literal annotations inside a sample SLO-as-code block and there is no network client call nearby. This is a real but minor publication concern because copied examples may retain external placeholders.

low_findings.title: Sample Templates Interpolate Values Without Validation Guidance
low_findings.description: Sample PromQL and HTML report templates interpolate service and period values directly. If copied into production with untrusted values, users should validate metric labels and escape generated HTML.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible in sample code, but the file is documentation and not an executable package. Risk depends on whether users copy the sample into production without validation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sample Templates Interpolate Values Without Validation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: Sample PromQL and HTML report templates interpolate service and period values directly. If copied into production with untrusted values, users should validate metric labels and escape generated HTML.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible in sample code, but the file is documentation and not an executable package. Risk depends on whether users copy the sample into production without validation.

content.value_statement: Teams often collect metrics but lack clear reliability targets and decision rules. This skill helps define SLOs, build error budget monitoring, and connect reliability signals to engineering decisions.
actual_capabilities: Designs SLO frameworks for service tiers, user journeys, and business impact.
Suggests SLIs for availability, latency, error rate, throughput, and quality.
Provides error budget calculations and burn-rate alerting patterns.
Outlines Prometheus recording rules and Grafana dashboard structures.
Creates reporting, decision-making, and governance templates for SLO programs.
limitations: Does not connect directly to live telemetry or monitoring systems.
Requires users to validate metric names, labels, and thresholds for their environment.
Example code is illustrative and needs production hardening before deployment.
Cannot replace stakeholder agreement on reliability targets and priorities.

use_cases.title: Launch SLOs for a Production API
use_cases.description: Define availability, latency, and error-rate objectives with error budget alerts for a customer-facing API.

use_cases.title: Standardize Reliability Reviews
use_cases.description: Create recurring reports and review templates that connect incidents, budgets, and release decisions.

use_cases.title: Align Reliability With Product Planning
use_cases.description: Translate service reliability data into prioritization guidance for features, toil, and corrective work.

prompt_templates.title: Define First SLOs
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define initial SLOs for a service. Ask for the service purpose, users, current metrics, incident history, and business impact before recommending targets.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Service Level Indicators
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these service metrics and recommend SLIs for availability, latency, and errors. Explain which metrics represent user experience and which should not drive alerts.

prompt_templates.title: Create Error Budget Alerts
prompt_templates.prompt: Design multi-window burn-rate alerts for this SLO target and measurement window. Include alert severity, routing intent, and when each alert should page or create a ticket.

prompt_templates.title: Build an SLO Governance Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an SLO governance process for multiple teams. Include ownership, review cadence, error budget policy, release decision rules, and stakeholder reporting.

output_examples.input: A payments API needs practical reliability objectives for launch readiness.
output: Recommended SLOs for availability, latency, and successful payment attempts.
A rationale that links each objective to customer impact and service tier.
An error budget policy with alert thresholds and release guidance.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants monthly SLO reporting for several services.
output: A report outline covering target performance, budget burn, incidents, trends, and actions.
A review agenda that assigns owners and captures decisions.
Recommendations for improving weak or noisy measurements.

output_examples.input: A team has dashboards but no clear alerting rules for reliability risk.
output: Burn-rate alert recommendations for fast and slow budget consumption.
Guidance for separating paging alerts from ticket-based follow-up.
Checks for validating that alerts reflect user-visible impact.

best_practices: Start with user journeys and business impact before selecting numeric targets.
Validate SLIs against real telemetry and incident history before enforcing policies.
Use error budgets to guide release, toil, and reliability investment decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not copy generic 99.9 percent targets without measuring current service behavior.
Do not page on every metric breach when the signal is not user-visible.
Do not treat SLOs as engineering-only documents without product and stakeholder agreement.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create SLO frameworks, SLI definitions, error budget policies, dashboards, alerts, reports, and review processes.

faq.question: Does it integrate with Prometheus or Grafana automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and example structures that users must adapt and apply in their own monitoring systems.

faq.question: Can it choose SLO targets without service data?
faq.answer: It can suggest starting points, but reliable targets require telemetry, incident history, and stakeholder agreement.

faq.question: Is this useful for non-API services?
faq.answer: Yes. The playbook includes concepts for APIs, data pipelines, batch systems, and reliability governance.

faq.question: How should teams use the example code?
faq.answer: Treat examples as templates. Validate metric labels, replace placeholder links, and harden generated reports before production use.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
